segments.s463-g41: به پیروزی آمد is an idiomatic past action: the verb آمد (simple past 'came') takes the goal phrase به پیروزی ('to/into victory' = 'achieved victory'). بران is an archaic contracted prepositional + demonstrative (بر + آن = 'on that' / 'upon that') and directly premodifies رزمگاه ('battlefield'); the whole segment thus states that victory was achieved on that specific battlefield. The subject is omitted (typical in Persian) and is understood from context.
segments.s463-g42: کجا پیش بود is a rhetorical interrogative: کجا ('where') is fronted, پیش here functions adverbially as 'before/previously', and بود is the past-tense copula ('had been' / 'was'). آن گزیده سپاه ('that chosen/elite army') is the noun phrase being asked about; گزیده is an adjectival past participle/ adjective 'chosen, select' modifying سپاه. The word order emphasizes the question ('Where, previously, was that chosen army?').
segments.s463-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of' introducing the cause. آواز اسپان is a noun phrase 'the sound/cry of the horses' (آواز = sound/voice + اسپان = horses) and بانگ سران is coordinated with و: 'and the cry of the chiefs/leaders' (بانگ = shout/cry, سران = leaders). Word order here is compacted poetic genitive without the ezāfe.
segments.s463-g26: چرنگیدن is a verbal/onomatopoeic form describing the action/sound made (here used of weapons); it functions as the predicate for the following noun phrase. گرزهای گران is a plural noun phrase 'the heavy maces' (گرزهای = maces, گران = heavy/weighty). The verb precedes the noun phrase in this poetic line, giving 'the heavy maces (did) چرنگیدن' — i.e., they clanged/swayed.
segments.s463-g27: تو گفتی is addressed to 'you' with گفتی in the (poetic) past sense 'you said' or 'you declared'. زمین کوه is a compact pair 'earth and mountain' (parallel nouns). جنگی شدست is a predicative phrase: جنگی 'warlike/became a battlefield' and شدست is the archaic poetic perfect/past 'has become' (literally 'became it is').
segments.s463-g28: ز introduces a cause/agent 'from' or 'because of' and گرد here means 'dust' or 'a cloud of dust' (ز گرد آسمان = from the dust of the sky). روی is 'face' and زنگی is an adjectival/nominal quality 'darkened/dusky' (not a personal name here). شدست again is the poetic past 'became/has become.' The clause describes faces darkening because of sky-dust.
segments.s463-g29: به یک هفته is a temporal expression 'in/within one week' (به marking the time-span). گردان is the noun 'a troop/unit' and پرخاشجوی is an attributive adjective 'aggressive, belligerent.' Together they form 'a troop of fierce fighters.'
segments.s463-g30: به روی اندر آورده بودند روی is a compact poetic description of direction and action: به روی 'toward the face/direction of', اندر 'in/into', آورده بودند is past perfect/pluperfect 'they had brought/turned', and the final روی ('face') resumes the object. Overall it means 'they had turned their faces toward (the scene).' آورده بودند is the past perfect auxiliary construction indicating completed action.
segments.s463-g31: بهشتم (poetic/compacted prepositional phrase referring to the sky/heaven) plus برآمد 'arose' gives the verb phrase 'there arose.' یکی تیره گرد is the noun phrase 'a single dark cloud/whirl of dust' (یکی = one/a certain, تیره = dark, گرد = dust/cloud). The order is typical poetic noun grouping after the verb.
segments.s463-g32: بران سان is a compound adverbial 'upon that / in such a way' (بران = thereon/thereof, سان = like/as). که introduces the result clause 'that' and خورشید شد لاژورد states the result: خورشید 'the sun', شد 'became', لاژورد 'lapis-lazuli/azure' — poetically 'the sun turned lapis-blue.'
segments.s463-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause places subject (خورشید) before a past simple verb (برزد), an archaic past form meaning 'rose/appeared'; سر از کوه is a prepositional phrase 'its head from/out of the mountain' (سر as 'top/head' + از + noun). The coordinating و links the noun راغ to the scene — here a poetic noun 'longing' added to the image.
segments.s463-g2: زمین شد is a past copular construction ('the earth became'); به کردار is a circumstantial phrase 'in the manner/likeness of' (به + noun), followed by the modifier زرین چراغ (an adjective + noun) forming the simile 'like a golden lamp'. The verb شد marks change of state.
segments.s463-g3: جهاندار دارا is a nominative phrase (title + name/apposition) serving as subject. سپه follows as the direct object and برگرفت is the past transitive verb 'took up/raised' (بر + گرفت as a single past-form verb), so the clause is Subject + Object + Verb (common in poetry).
segments.s463-g4: جهان is subject; چادر قیر is the object (a tar tent/cover). The light preposition بر before سرگرفت combines with the verb سرگرفت (here 'seized/took on') to yield the compound idea 'put on/assumed upon the head' — a figurative way to say the world was covered by a tar cloak. The verb is past and the construction is idiomatic.
segments.s463-g5: بیاورد is a past transitive verb 'brought' (third person). لشکر is its direct object. The preposition ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from' and modifies رود فرات ('the river Euphrates') which follows in the genitive-like sequence 'رود فرات'. Word order is Verb + Object + origin phrase, a common flexible order in epic verse.
segments.s463-g6: به هامون marks location/direction 'onto/to the plain'. The verbless verblike clause places سپه (the host) followed by بیش بود (literally 'was more'), a pseudo-comparative: X بیش بود از Y = 'X was more than Y'. از نبات supplies the standard of comparison 'than the vegetation'. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s463-g7: سکندر is the subject. چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. کامد is an archaic present/past form meaning 'came/was coming' (here reported as the content of what was heard), and سپاه is the theme of that hearing (the arriving army). The sequence is 'When Alexander heard (that) the army was coming.'
segments.s463-g8: بزد کوس is a short past clause 'he struck the war-drum' (بزد = past of زدن). The coordinating و links the next action: آورد لشکر به راه — آورد (past 'brought'/'moved') with لشکر as object and به راه ('onto the road') as direction; together 'he beat the drum and marshaled/brought his army onto the road.'
segments.s463-g17: به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the front of the army' (به + پیش). آوریدند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they brought' and پیِل is the direct object placed after the verb for poetic word order: 'they brought (آوریدند) an elephant (پیل) to the army's front (به پیش سپاه)'.
segments.s463-g18: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد = become). به کردار is a classical simile-marker 'in the manner/appearance of' and is followed by دریای نیل 'the sea of the Nile' as the likeness. The whole clause means the scene looked as vast as the Nile.
segments.s463-g19: سواران جنگ is 'the riders/horsemen of war' (noun + genitive/attributive). از پس here means 'from the rear / behind' and پیش after پیل places the elephant 'in front'. The conjunction و links the two positions: horsemen behind and elephant ahead.
segments.s463-g20: همه برگرفته is a predicate: 'all were seized / had seized' (برگرفته used as perfect/adjectival participle). دل از جان خویش is an idiomatic genitive construction 'the heart from one's life' with خویش expressed by the elements خوی + ش (possessive); it means their hearts seemed to leave their lives — a poetic way to say they were panic-stricken or overwhelmed.
segments.s463-g21: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said'. هوا خون خروشد همی is the reported clause: خروشد is an old/poetic 3rd-person form meaning 'will roar' or 'roars', and همی is the classical particle that gives a continuous or emphatic aspect ('would/indeed'). The sequence expresses a prediction: 'you said the air would roar (with) blood.'
segments.s463-g22: زمین از خروشش بجوشد همی is a consequence clause: زمین 'the earth' + از خروشش 'from its roar' (خروشش = خروش + possessive -ش) + بجوشد 'will boil/seethe' (third-person singular, poetic present/future). همی again marks the continuous/emphatic sense. Grammatically, the possessive -ش ties the 'roar' back to the previous clause.
segments.s463-g23: ز بس is a poetic causal/intensity construction 'because of so much' or 'from so great'. ناله بوق و هندی درای lists the sounds: ناله 'moan/cry', بوق 'horn-blast', و 'and', هندی 'Indian' (here indicating an Indian instrument or style); درای is an older/poetic form attached to هندی and here denotes possession/quality of sound. The whole phrase describes massive, overwhelming noises.
segments.s463-g24: همی at the start gives an ongoing/emphatic sense. کوه را with را marks the mountain as the affected topic. دل برآمد ز جای is a metaphorical clause: 'the mountain's heart rose from its place' (برآمد past 'arose/issued forth', ز جای 'from its place'), meaning the mountain trembled or was moved. The object marker را highlights the dramatic effect on the mountain.
segments.s463-g33: بپوشید is the preterite-form of پوشیدن with the classical/preverbal ب‑prefix and here means 'was covered/concealed' (an impersonal/third‑singular use). دیدار ایران سپاه is a noun phrase where دیدار ('appearance/sight') is modified by the genitive sequence ایران سپاه ('the army of Iran' — Persian places the possessor after the possessed). Together: 'the sight of Iran's army was concealed.'
segments.s463-g34: گریزان is a participial/adverbial modifier meaning 'fleeing' (manner), placed before the verb. برفتند is the past tense 3rd plural 'they went/left'. از آن رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' giving the point of departure.
segments.s463-g35: سپاه سکندر is a genitive construction 'the army of Sīkandar' (head before modifier). پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. اندر دمان is a locative phrase 'in/within the skirts/outskirts' (دمان here = the camp's rim or outskirts), giving the location of the army.
segments.s463-g36: یکی ... و دیگری ... is a correlative pair 'one ... and the other ...' used to describe two groups or persons. پرغم is an adjective/adverbial 'full of sorrow' describing the first, and شادمان 'joyful/rejoicing' describes the other.
segments.s463-g37: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بشد is the classical past of شدن used here to indicate his arrival/coming ('he came/arrived'); تا with the meaning 'as far as' introduces the limit of his advance, followed by لب رودبار 'the river‑bank/river‑valley' (لب = brink, رودبار = river valley/place by the river).
segments.s463-g38: بکشتند is past 3rd plural 'they killed' (the preposed ب‑ is the classical past marker). ز ایرانیان indicates the victims 'from among the Iranians' (ز = 'of/from' in epic diction), and بی‌شمار is a postpositive adjective 'countless/numerous' modifying ایرانیان.
segments.s463-g39: سپاه is the subject 'the army'. از لب رود is a prepositional phrase 'from the river-bank' (از = from, لب = brink, رود = river). برگاشتند is past 3rd plural 'they returned/withdrew' (classical form meaning 'came back/left').
segments.s463-g40: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'that'/'so that'. رود is the object 'the river' and بگذاشتند (past 3rd plural of گذاشتن with the preverb ب‑) here means 'they left/abandoned' (i.e., 'he ordered that they leave the river/that they depart from the river').
segments.s463-g9: دو لشکر is the noun phrase 'two armies' (دو = two, لشکر = host/army). که introduces a relative clause modifying لشکر. آن را is a demonstrative plus the object/definite marker (را) resuming the antecedent; کرانه نبود is a predicate: کرانه 'shore/limit' + نبود, the past of بودن 'to be', here meaning 'there was no limit'. The word order places the demonstrative object (آن را) before the predicate (کرانه نبود), a common poetic/archaic pattern.
segments.s463-g10: چو ... نبود is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' (archaic/poetic), X اندر زمانه نبود = 'X was not in the age/world'. اندر is an older preposition meaning 'in', and نبود is past 'was not' — together meaning 'in the time before X existed' or 'before X's time'.
segments.s463-g11: ز ... و ز is a poetic repetition of the preposition ز 'from' (or 'by'), used here to list sources: ساز 'instruments' (here musical instruments used in an army), و 'and', گردان 'columns/troops'. هر دو گروه literally 'each two group' is a poetic way to indicate 'both groups/units' (هر with دو emphasizes the duality), so together: 'from instruments and from the marching columns, both groups'.
segments.s463-g12: زمین 'the earth' is the subject; همچو دریا is a simile 'like a sea'. The short word بد here is a poetic/archaic form related to 'بُود' (past of بودن) meaning 'was' or 'became' — i.e. 'the earth became like a sea'. و links the following noun phrase; گرد here is used as a noun 'dust' (گردِ = dust cloud) and کوه 'mountain' completes the phrase 'dust about the mountain(s)'.
segments.s463-g13: ز introduces sources again ('from'). خفتان is an archaic/poetic word here; its exact lexical force is difficult and likely refers to an item or instrument associated with the army (the manuscript/usage is rare), so I mark it as uncertain. وز here is a fused poetic variant of و + ز ('and from'). خنجر هندوان = 'the daggers of the Indians' (genitive construction: خنجر + هندوان). Grammatically the line is a list of sources/forces producing the tumult.
segments.s463-g14: ز بالا 'from on high/above' (بالا = above); و اسپ = 'and horses/steeds' (اسپ = horse). وز again functions as the poetic 'and from'; برگستوان is derived from برگشتن 'to return' and here functions as a noun-like verbal form meaning 'the returning' or 'those who return' (a participial/nominal use). The phrase continues the catalog of sources/movements associated with the armies. Poetic order allows these items to run together without additional particles.
segments.s463-g15: دو رویه سپه: دو modifies رویه (here 'two sides/faces' or 'both flanks' of the army — رویه in epic usage can mean 'side' or 'face'), سپه 'host/army'. برکشیدند is past, 3rd person plural 'they lifted/raised/drew up' (here 'they drew up' the lines). صف 'rank/line' is the object/complement. Word order places the verb before the object, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s463-g16: ز خنجر 'from daggers'; همی before the past verb یافت gives a repeated/habitual or continuous past sense ('would find' / 'kept finding'); یافت is past of یافتن 'to find/receive'. خورشید تف is a compact poetic image: خورشید (here 'the sun' or 'sun's splendor') and تف 'spit' (an image of being spat upon or struck); together the line personifies the sun as if struck/spat on by the daggers' flashes. Grammatically this is an object-experiencer construction with poetic ellipsis.

sentences.s463-s21: Victory came on that battlefield; where had that chosen army been before?
sentences.s463-s13: From the neighing of the horses and the cries of the chiefs, the heavy maces rang/clashed.
sentences.s463-s14: You said the earth and the mountains had become a field of battle; from the dust of the sky their faces had darkened.
sentences.s463-s15: Within a single week a troop of fierce fighters had turned their faces toward (the battle).
sentences.s463-s16: From the heavens there rose a single dark cloud, so that the sun became lapis-blue.
sentences.s463-s1: When the sun lifted its head above the mountain and longing, the earth became like a golden lamp.
sentences.s463-s2: Dara, the world-ruler, raised his host; the world put on a tar tent upon its head.
sentences.s463-s3: He brought an army from the river Euphrates; on the plain the host was more numerous than the vegetation.
sentences.s463-s4: When Alexander heard that the army was coming, he struck the war-drum and brought the army onto the road.
sentences.s463-s9: They brought an elephant to the front of the army; the world became like the sea of the Nile.
sentences.s463-s10: The horsemen of war were behind and the elephant in front; all had their hearts seized from their lives.
sentences.s463-s11: You said the air would roar with blood; indeed the earth would boil from that roar.
sentences.s463-s12: Because of so much lamentation, horn-blasts and Indian (war-)sounds, the mountain's heart rose from its place.
sentences.s463-s17: The sight/appearance of Iran's army was hidden; they fled from that battlefield.
sentences.s463-s18: Alexander's army then, within the camp's outskirts, one part sorrowful and the other rejoicing.
sentences.s463-s19: Alexander reached as far as the river-bank and they slew countless Iranians.
sentences.s463-s20: The army withdrew from the river-bank; he ordered that they leave the river.
sentences.s463-s5: Two boundless armies — they had no limit — before Alexander was yet in the world.
sentences.s463-s6: From instruments and from the marching companies — both ranks — they made the earth like a sea, and dust about the mountains.
sentences.s463-s7: From trumpets (or banners) and from the Indians' daggers; from above and from steeds, and from returning ranks.
sentences.s463-s8: On both sides the host drew up its ranks; from daggers the sun itself seemed struck (its splendor spat).
